1: The command to take twelve stones from the Jordan 'from the place where the priests' feet stood firm' carries sacramental significance. The stones are taken from the exact location where YHWH's presence accomplished the miracle — the holiest ground in the crossing. They become portable witnesses to the power of divine presence. The NT sacraments function analogously: water, bread, and wine taken from ordinary creation are placed in the context of Christ's salvific action and become memorials of the event in which he stood firm for his people. The materials themselves do not contain power; they point to the event in which divine power was manifest.
2: The twelve men — one from each tribe — are the representative witnesses whose stones become the memorial. The apostolic pattern is precisely this: twelve representative witnesses of the resurrection are sent to all tribes and nations with the testimony. 'You will be my witnesses in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth' (Acts 1:8). The twelve apostles are the NT equivalent of the twelve stone-bearers: representative members of the new Israel who carry the memorial of the crossing (the resurrection testimony) from the site of the miracle to all the nations. The memorial is not a stone monument but a living community of witnesses.
3: The stones are to be set up at 'the place where you lodge tonight' — Gilgal, the base camp of the conquest. The Christological reading: the memorial of Christ's crossing through death is established at the church's base camp, the place where the community returns and prepares for each new campaign. The Lord's Supper is this base-camp memorial: 'Do this in remembrance of me... For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes' (1 Corinthians 11:24-26). The Eucharist is the Gilgal stone circle — the constantly-renewed memorial of the crossing accomplished, setting the camp of the church in its position for the ongoing conquest.
4: Joshua's calling of the twelve appointed men to carry the stones back from the Jordan riverbed types Christ's calling of the twelve apostles: chosen in advance (Ephesians 1:4; John 15:16), commissioned for a specific memorial task (Matthew 28:19-20), and sent back into the world (the Jordan's crossing-place) to carry the testimony of the resurrection. 'As the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you' (John 20:21). The twelve men called by Joshua had been pre-appointed (Joshua 3:12) — as the twelve apostles were pre-appointed and known to Christ before the resurrection (John 6:70; Joshua 13:18).
5: 'Pass on before the Ark of the LORD your God into the midst of the Jordan, and take up each of you a stone upon his shoulder' — the command to carry a stone on the shoulder has a specific NT echo: 'whoever does not take his cross and follow me is not worthy of me' (Matthew 10:38). The stones carried from the Jordan riverbed on the shoulders of the twelve men type the cross carried on the shoulders of Christ's disciples. The stone (the memorial burden) is carried out of the place of death (the Jordan riverbed) into the place of life (Gilgal) — as the cross (the burden of discipleship) is carried out of the old life into the new. Each man carries one stone; each disciple carries one cross.
6: 'That this may be a sign among you. When your children ask in time to come, "What do those stones mean to you?"' — the catechetical function of the memorial stones types the NT's catechesis around the Lord's Supper: 'For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes' (1 Corinthians 11:26). The children's question ('What do these stones mean?') is the structural equivalent of the new believer's inquiry at the Lord's Table: 'What does this bread and cup mean?' Both answers are the same type of answer: 'On this day, YHWH/Christ acted, and we live because he crossed through death for us.' The sacrament is a visible question-prompt for the oral proclamation of the saving event.
7: 'These stones shall be to the people of Israel a memorial forever' — the 'forever' memorial of the Jordan crossing types the 'until he comes' of the Lord's Supper. Both memorials are temporally bounded: the Jordan stones were a memorial until the stones themselves were lost or the community dispersed; the Lord's Supper is 'until he comes.' The eschatological horizon of the Eucharist — anticipating the fulfillment when the memorial is superseded by the presence of the one remembered — corresponds to the Jordan stones anticipating the full inheritance of the land. When the land is fully possessed, the memorial of the crossing becomes less urgent; when Christ returns, the memorial supper is replaced by the feast itself (Revelation 19:9).
8: 'The people of Israel did just as Joshua commanded' — the obedience-formula that marks successful completion in Joshua types the church's obedience to Christ's commands: baptism and the Lord's Supper practiced 'as he commanded.' 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit, teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you' (Matthew 28:19-20). The stone-memorial was established by Israel's obedient execution of Joshua's command; the sacramental memorials of the church are established by the community's obedient execution of Christ's command. Sacraments without Christ's command are empty rituals; with his command, they are covenant acts.
9: The twelve stones set in the Jordan riverbed itself — the underwater monument — types the 'hidden' memorial of Christ's work in the depths of death. Christ's descent into death left a permanent mark: 'When he ascended on high he led a host of captives' (Ephesians 4:8, citing Psalms 68:18). The Jordan riverbed monument was visible only at low water, a hidden memorial of the miracle. Christ's descent into Hades (1 Peter 3:18-20) is this underwater monument — the memorial of his victory in the very depths of death, visible to those with eyes to see but hidden from ordinary perception. 'They are there to this day' — the victory accomplished in death's depths is permanent.
10: 'The priests who bore the Ark remained standing in the midst of the Jordan until everything was finished that the LORD commanded Joshua to tell the people' — the priests stood until 'everything was finished.' Jesus's cry from the cross — 'It is finished' (tetelestai, John 19:30) — is the definitive fulfillment of this priestly standing until completion. As the priests could not leave the Jordan until all Israel had crossed, Christ could not descend from the cross until the atonement was complete. 'The people hurried and crossed' — as the urgency in the Jordan crossing drove Israel forward, so the urgency of the gospel drives the church's mission. The priests stood; Christ stayed on the cross; not until 'everything was finished' could the holy act be declared complete.
11: The Ark crossing last — after all the people, as the rear guard — types Christ as both forerunner and rear guard. He went before into death (forerunner, Hebrews 6:20), and he comes behind the church as the one who guards its back. 'The LORD will go before you, and the God of Israel will be your rear guard' (Isaiah 52:12). As the Ark crossed last and the waters returned behind it (Joshua 4:18), so Christ's ascension sealed off death behind him — the resurrection body cannot die again (Romans 6:9), and the way opened by Christ through death remains open for all who follow. The return of the waters did not trap Israel; it sealed the completed crossing. Christ's ascension does not close the way; it confirms the crossing is accomplished.
12: The Transjordanian warriors crossing 'armed in front of the people of Israel' — fulfilling their obligation before returning to their own inheritance — types the missionary church crossing into the world 'armed' with the gospel before entering the final rest. 'For we do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the cosmic powers over this present darkness' (Ephesians 6:12). The spiritual armor of Ephesians 6 corresponds to the warriors' military equipment at the Jordan: not physical weapons but the weapons of truth, righteousness, faith, salvation, the word of God. The church is always the Transjordanian warrior — already inheriting in Christ (Ephesians 2:6), but still fighting for the full possession of the land.
13: The 40,000 armed warriors crossing the Jordan types the great multitude who pass through the waters of death in Christ — 'the great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages, standing before the throne' (Revelation 7:9). Forty is the number of wilderness testing and preparation; 40,000 (forty × one thousand) types the complete number of those prepared and consecrated by the wilderness experience who finally enter the inheritance. The complete number of the redeemed is not yet known — 'a great multitude that no one could number' — but it will be the full army of the Lord, all armed and ready for the final conquest.
14: 'On that day the LORD exalted Joshua in the sight of all Israel, and they feared him just as they had feared Moses, all the days of his life' — the exaltation of Joshua after the Jordan miracle types the exaltation of Christ after the resurrection. 'This Jesus God raised up... Let all the house of Israel therefore know for certain that God has made him both Lord and Christ' (Acts 2:32,36). The people's awe before Joshua ('they feared him as they had feared Moses') types the church's posture before the risen Christ — reverent faith that recognizes divine authority confirmed by the miracle of new life. The resurrection is the Jordan miracle that establishes Christ's authority for all who witness it.
15: The chain of command — YHWH speaks to Joshua, Joshua commands the priests — maintained throughout the crossing narrative types the apostolic chain in the NT: Christ commissions the apostles, the apostles commission the church. 'As the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you' (John 20:21). The theological point is that authority flows through a specific chain of commission; the priests did not act on their own authority, and the apostles did not act on their own authority. Every step in the Jordan crossing was authorized by YHWH through Joshua; every step in the mission of the church is authorized by the Father through Christ through the apostolic tradition.
16: 'Command the priests bearing the Ark of the testimony to come up out of the Jordan' — the priests could not exit the Jordan until commanded. Christ's resurrection was also not a self-initiated event but was declared by the Father: 'God raised him from the dead' (Acts 13:30; Romans 4:24; Galatians 1:1). The Son did not raise himself; the Father raised the Son (John 5:21). The priests could not leave the Jordan on their own initiative; they waited for the command that would come through Joshua from YHWH. The dependence of the Son on the Father's command-to-rise types the structure of resurrection as divine act, not human achievement — 'it was not possible for him to be held by it' (Acts 2:24), not because he was too powerful but because death had no claim on the righteous one.
17: 'So Joshua commanded the priests' — the moment of command is the moment of the resurrection's announcement. As Joshua's command set the priests in motion out of the Jordan, the Father's command (the divine decree of resurrection) set the Son in motion out of death. The 'command' is the operative category: the resurrection is not a natural resuscitation but a divine decree, YHWH overturning death's verdict on the one who was 'declared to be the Son of God in power according to the Spirit of holiness by his resurrection from the dead' (Romans 1:4). The priests emerged from the Jordan commanded; Christ emerged from death declared.
18: 'As soon as the priests bearing the Ark of the covenant of the LORD came up from the midst of the Jordan, and the soles of the priests' feet were lifted up on dry ground, the waters of the Jordan returned to their place' — the moment the priests' feet left the water, the miracle reversed. The resurrection's consequence: death's door closed behind Christ (Romans 6:9, 'death no longer has dominion over him'). The Jordan flooding back types the definitive closure of death's claim on the risen Christ. 'He died once for all to sin' — the Jordan can only be crossed once; death can only be endured once by the atoning Christ. The return of the waters does not threaten the people who have crossed; it confirms that the crossing is complete and cannot be undone.
19: The arrival at Gilgal on the tenth of Nisan — the day of Passover lamb-selection — places the Jordan crossing in precise liturgical alignment with the Passover. The Christological reading of this calendar: Jesus, the Passover Lamb, entered Jerusalem on the tenth of Nisan (if Palm Sunday = Nisan 10) for his final Passover. The convergence of Joshua's Jordan-arrival on Nisan 10 with Jesus's Jerusalem-arrival on (approximately) Nisan 10 is not arbitrary; both events are moving within the liturgical calendar that YHWH appointed. The crossing of the Jordan is the beginning of the Passover week's consummation — as Jesus's entry into Jerusalem begins the week of his own Passover sacrifice.
20: The erection of the twelve Jordan stones at Gilgal types the apostolic foundation of the church. 'Built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone' (Ephesians 2:20). The stones taken from the Jordan miracle site are the testimony of those who passed through — as the apostles' foundation role derives from their having been present at the resurrection, the definitive Jordan-crossing. Gilgal was the base camp from which all the campaigns of the conquest were launched; the apostolic foundation (the twelve stones) is the base camp from which the church's mission proceeds. No campaign moved without returning to Gilgal; no faithful ministry departs from the apostolic testimony.
21: 'When your children ask their fathers in times to come, "What do these stones mean?"' — the pedagogical memorial points forward to the church's catechesis. The Great Commission includes 'teaching them to observe all that I have commanded you' (Matthew 28:20) — the memorial that triggers the catechetical question. In Christian practice, the physical acts of baptism and the Lord's Supper serve as the children's question-provoking memorial: 'What does this water mean? What does this bread and cup mean?' The answer is always the same Jordan-testimony: 'YHWH divided the waters; Christ divided death — and we crossed through safely because of what he did.'
22: 'You shall let your children know, "Israel passed over this Jordan on dry ground"' — the specific content of the catechetical answer is always the historical event, not a principle or a feeling. 'Israel passed over' — specific people, specific water, specific date. The NT's catechetical content is equally specific: 'that Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures, that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures' (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). The Jordan testimony is not 'YHWH is powerful in general' but 'on this day, at this place, he did this specific thing.' Christian faith is anchored in historical particularity, as Joshua's faith was anchored in the specific day of the Jordan crossing.
23: 'For the LORD your God dried up the waters of the Jordan for you until you passed over, as the LORD your God did to the Red Sea, which he dried up for us until we passed over' — the Jordan crossing interpreted through the Red Sea crossing is the OT pattern of reading new saving acts in light of prior ones. The NT reads Christ's cross and resurrection in light of both: 'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed' (1 Corinthians 5:7); 'For I do not want you to be unaware, brothers, that our fathers were all under the cloud, and all passed through the sea, and all were baptized into Moses' (1 Corinthians 10:1-2). The catechism of salvation always connects events: sea → Jordan → Christ's baptism-death-resurrection. Each event illuminates the next.
24: 'So that all the peoples of the earth may know that the hand of the LORD is mighty, and so that you may fear the LORD your God forever' — the universal purpose of the Jordan crossing (all peoples to know YHWH) is fulfilled in the Great Commission: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations' (Matthew 28:19). The 'mighty hand' of YHWH that stopped the Jordan is the same hand revealed in Christ's resurrection. Peter's Pentecost sermon makes this explicit: 'This Jesus... you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men. God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it' (Acts 2:23-24). The Jordan miracle was for all nations to witness YHWH's power; the resurrection is the definitive 'all nations' revelation of divine power over death.
